does: This paper proposes a novel exploration scheme for online reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF) that adaptively collects preference data to simultaneously refine reward models and policies efficiently. The core contribution is identifying a fundamental flaw in existing optimism-based exploration methods: they gather comparisons that fail to reduce the most decision-relevant uncertainties in reward differences, resulting in linear regret over exponentially long horizons. To address this, the authors develop a new exploration algorithm that strategically directs preference queries toward reducing uncertainty in reward differences most critical to policy improvement. Under a multi-armed bandit formulation of RLHF, they establish improved regret bounds of order $T^{(β+1)/(β+2)}$, demonstrating better sample efficiency than prior approaches. The analysis reveals that exploring toward policy-relevant uncertainty substantially outperforms undirected exploration strategies.
connects: This work builds on the established RLHF paradigm that has become central to large language model alignment, extending classical reinforcement learning theory into the online human preference learning setting. It responds to and improves upon existing optimism-based exploration algorithms in RLHF, which were adapted from general exploration principles in bandit problems and reinforcement learning. The paper draws on foundational concepts from active learning and uncertainty reduction, particularly the idea of seeking information most relevant to decision-making rather than global uncertainty reduction. It connects to information-theoretic approaches in exploration and classical regret analysis in multi-armed bandits, adapting these frameworks to the specific structure of preference-based reward learning where comparisons between actions matter most for policy optimization.
enables: This work opens avenues for more sample-efficient RLHF systems, potentially reducing the annotation burden for aligning large language models with human preferences. By establishing tighter regret bounds and demonstrating the importance of policy-relevant exploration, the research enables future work on adaptive preference elicitation strategies that could scale RLHF to more complex domains. The framework suggests how to design exploration policies that balance refining global reward models with focusing on comparisons that directly improve decision-making, applicable beyond LLMs to any domain using preference feedback. Follow-up research could extend these bounds to function approximation settings, investigate exploration-exploitation trade-offs in more complex policy spaces, and develop practical algorithms that implement policy-relevant uncertainty reduction at scale for realistic language model training.
